description: The most medically significant snake in the Amazon basin and the leading cause of snakebite fatalities in Brazil. Largely terrestrial and nocturnal, inhabiting lowland rainforest, forest edges, and riparian corridors. Highly adaptable and frequently encountered near human settlements.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #49001

description: A vivid green arboreal pit viper of Amazonian rainforest canopy and understorey. Primarily nocturnal, coiling on branches 1–4 metres above ground. Despite its arboreal habits, it is responsible for a significant number of snakebite incidents in the region.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #539411

description: A robust and secretive pit viper of primary Amazonian forest. Largely terrestrial and nocturnal, sheltering beneath leaf litter and fallen logs. Less frequently encountered than Bothrops atrox but equally dangerous due to potent hemotoxic venom.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #30796
credit_line: © Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

description: A slender, arboreal pit viper distinguished by its strongly keeled scales and speckled brown-and-tan pattern. Primarily nocturnal, hunting in low vegetation and on the forest floor. Reported as Bothrops castelnaudi in older Carajás literature.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30779

description: The largest venomous snake in the Americas and the longest pit viper in the world. A strictly nocturnal ambush predator of primary rainforest, relying on exceptional leaf-litter camouflage to ambush small mammals. A strong indicator of intact primary habitat.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #73840

description: A little-known coral snake of Amazonian forest, identified by a distinctive banding pattern. Largely fossorial and rarely encountered. Confirmed from the Carajás National Forest in the 2015–2023 survey period.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30517

description: A slender, boldly banded coral snake with red, black, and white rings. Secretive and largely fossorial. Bites are uncommon but potentially life-threatening due to potent neurotoxic venom.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #30498

description: The Pará coral snake — a species with restricted distribution centred on the Brazilian state of Pará. Confirmed from Carajás National Forest in the 2015–2023 survey. Very rarely encountered. Ecology poorly known.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30526

description: A semi-aquatic coral snake of Amazonian forest and wetland margins. The Carajás population was formerly referred to as Micrurus spixii martiusi. Feeds primarily on elongate prey including other snakes and caecilians.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; subspecies collapsed to species · iNaturalist taxon #30517
credit_line: © Vincent A. Vos via GBIF (CC BY)

description: An aquatic coral snake closely associated with rivers and streams. Distinguished from other coral snakes by its predominantly aquatic habits and association with riparian habitats. Confirmed from the 2015–2023 Carajás National Forest survey.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30551
credit_line: © Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; taxonomic update per Reptile Database · iNaturalist taxon #1439538

description: A slender nocturnal arboreal snake specialised for extracting snails and slugs from their shells using its elongated lower jaw. One of the most frequently recorded species in night surveys at Carajás National Forest. Calm and slow-moving, relying on camouflage rather than flight.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30392
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30395
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #30378
credit_line: © Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30406
credit_line: © Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29111
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #30152

description: A small ground snake described from specimens collected in the Carajás region and considered endemic to the area. Associated with the open canga (ironstone outcrop) vegetation unique to the Serra dos Carajás plateau. One of the very few reptiles considered specific to this habitat. Very rarely encountered.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; taxonomy updated · iNaturalist taxon #539676
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; taxonomy updated · iNaturalist taxon #539691
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; taxonomy updated · iNaturalist taxon #539696

description: A semi-aquatic snake strongly associated with slow-moving water bodies, flooded forest margins, and riverside vegetation. Feeds primarily on fish and frogs. Keeled scales and dorsally positioned eyes are adaptations for an aquatic lifestyle.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29976

description: A remarkably slender arboreal snake with a disproportionately large head and enormous eyes adapted for nocturnal vision. Moves through vegetation with exceptional agility, bridging gaps between branches. Feeds almost exclusively on sleeping lizards and frogs. One of the most commonly encountered arboreal snakes during night surveys.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29873
credit_line: © Giovanni Alberto Chaves Portilla / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

description: A nocturnal cat-eyed snake of forest and riparian habitats, identifiable by its large vertical pupils and boldly banded pattern. Feeds primarily on frogs, lizards, and their eggs. One of the most frequently recorded snake species in the 2015–2023 Carajás National Forest survey.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29718

description: A slender, fast-moving brown vine snake of forest edges and open shrubby habitats. Less conspicuous than its green relative Oxybelis fulgidus. Diurnal, feeding primarily on lizards. Rear-fanged venom presents minimal risk to humans.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #83515
credit_line: © Thomaz de Carvalho Callado via GBIF (CC BY)

description: An extraordinarily slender, bright green arboreal snake that mimics a vine with remarkable fidelity. Diurnal and fast-moving, hunting lizards through active pursuit. Its rear-fanged venom presents minimal risk to humans. One of the most morphologically distinctive snakes of the region.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #29178

description: A nocturnal false coral snake of Amazonian forest. Distinguished from Oxyrhopus petolarius by its black head. Feeds on lizards and small mammals. Confirmed from Carajás National Forest in the 2015–2023 survey period.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29201

description: A boldly patterned snake that mimics venomous coral snakes with red, black, and cream banding. Primarily nocturnal and terrestrial, feeding on lizards and small mammals. One of the most commonly misidentified snakes in the region.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #146320
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; iNaturalist uses viridissima · iNaturalist taxon #29097
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29240
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #28942
credit_line: © Antônio Jorge Suzart Argôlo / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #28766
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30591
credit_line: © Anthony Mora Aguilar via GBIF (CC BY)

source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #28656
credit_line: © Vincent A. Vos via GBIF (CC BY)

source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #28662
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30007

description: A stocky terrestrial snake that mimics Bothrops pit vipers, flattening its head into a triangular shape when threatened. Feeds primarily on frogs. Despite its alarming defensive display, its rear-fanged venom presents little risk to humans.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #28031

description: A larger relative of Xenodon rabdocephalus with similar defensive mimicry behaviour. Associated with forest floor habitats. Recorded from the 1985 Cunha et al. survey of the Carajás region.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #115052
credit_line: © Diego Tirira from Quito, Ecuador / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #30045
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29953
credit_line: © Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

description: A primitive burrowing snake representing one of the most ancient snake lineages in South America. Boldly banded in red and black to mimic venomous coral snakes. Strictly fossorial, spending almost all of its life underground or beneath leaf litter. Feeds on elongate prey including caecilians and other snakes.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #32073
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #None
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #675260

description: One of the most widespread and recognisable snakes in the Americas. A powerful constrictor occupying habitats from dense rainforest to open savanna and canga. Primarily terrestrial but capable climbers. Feeds on birds and mammals up to the size of small deer.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29377
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29899
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29907
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29910
credit_line: © Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #27241
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29915
credit_line: © Rafaella Cevallos Larrea via GBIF (CC BY)

description: The Amazon Basin Emerald Tree Boa — a spectacular bright green arboreal boa with a white vertebral stripe and irregular lateral markings. Nocturnal, hunting birds and small mammals from branches. South-of-Amazon populations were formally split from Corallus caninus in 2009.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #672388

description: A highly variable arboreal boa found throughout Amazonia, with colour morphs ranging from yellow and orange to grey and brown. Strictly nocturnal, coiling on branches 2–5 metres above ground. An ambush predator of lizards, birds, and small mammals.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #32082
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29212
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #30335
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29315

description: Named for the spectacular iridescent sheen of its scales, which produces a rainbow effect in sunlight due to microscopic ridges acting as prisms. A semi-arboreal constrictor of humid Amazonian forest. Primarily nocturnal, feeding on small rodents, birds, and lizards.
source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #32237

description: A brilliantly iridescent green arboreal snake with striking metallic scales. Diurnal and fast-moving, hunting frogs and lizards in low vegetation and forest edges. Rear-fanged venom causes minimal symptoms in humans. Commonly encountered in riparian and edge habitats.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #29688
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #29397
credit_line: © E Santos Ortega via GBIF (CC BY)

source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; spelling updated · iNaturalist taxon #29395
credit_line: © Bernard DUPONT from FRANCE / Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA)

source_notes: UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #29334

description: One of the largest non-venomous snakes in the Neotropics, boldly marked with black and yellow. An agile diurnal predator equally at home on the ground and in the canopy. Highly alert and defensive when approached.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #28627

description: A large, predominantly yellow arboreal snake with black markings. Closely related to Spilotes pullatus but more strongly associated with the forest canopy. Both survey periods confirmed its presence at Carajás.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey; 2015–2023 Carajás National Forest survey · iNaturalist taxon #29672
UFSC thesis Table S1 (2015–2023 CNF survey) · iNaturalist taxon #33559
Cunha et al. 1985 Carajás reptile survey · iNaturalist taxon #28018
